reasons: mutable object reference with no guard
projectRisks.summary: Field 'GameState.instance' (GameState) has 1 writer(s) [1 unguarded] and 2 reader(s) across the project.

fixSuggestions.approach: Synchronize all read and write access to this field
fixSuggestions.cons: Blocking; must be applied consistently at every access site or the guarantee breaks

fixSuggestions.pros: Lock-free reads/writes of the reference itself
fixSuggestions.cons: Only makes the reference swap atomic, not mutations to the object it points to

reasons: mutable object reference with no guard
touched by a method that runs on its own thread (@Scheduled / message handler)
projectRisks.summary: Field 'GameState.phase' (GamePhase) has 7 writer(s) [7 unguarded] and 2 reader(s) across the project.

fixSuggestions.approach: Synchronize all read and write access to this field
fixSuggestions.cons: Blocking; must be applied consistently at every access site or the guarantee breaks

fixSuggestions.pros: Lock-free reads/writes of the reference itself
fixSuggestions.cons: Only makes the reference swap atomic, not mutations to the object it points to

reasons: touched by a method that runs on its own thread (@Scheduled / message handler)
projectRisks.summary: Field 'GameState.weaponsOnGround' (List<Weapon>) has 9 writer(s) [9 unguarded] and 14 reader(s) across the project.

fixSuggestions.approach: Replace with a concurrent collection (ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList, etc.)
fixSuggestions.pros: No manual locking; safe for concurrent iteration and modification
fixSuggestions.cons: Slightly higher memory/overhead than a plain collection; CopyOnWrite* is expensive for write-heavy workloads

fixSuggestions.approach: Synchronize every access site with a single shared lock
fixSuggestions.pros: Works with any collection type, easy to reason about
fixSuggestions.cons: All access serializes on one lock; can become a throughput bottleneck; easy to miss a call site

projectRisks.summary: Object reference 'instance' is written and later read without a visibility guarantee.

fixSuggestions.pros: Guarantees visibility of the latest write across threads with minimal overhead
fixSuggestions.cons: Does not make compound operations on the object atomic — only the reference assignment

fixSuggestions.approach: Make the field final and fully construct the object before publishing it
fixSuggestions.pros: Strongest guarantee; safe publication is automatic per the Java Memory Model
fixSuggestions.cons: Requires the object to be fully immutable or constructed in one shot, which isn't always possible

reasons: lazy initialization of 'instance' in GameState.getInstance() has no synchronization at all
two threads can simultaneously observe instance == null and both construct a new instance
projectRisks.summary: Method 'GameState.getInstance()' lazily initializes 'instance' with a plain null-check and assignment, with no locking whatsoever. Concurrent callers can race past the null check together, each constructing their own instance — silently breaking the singleton guarantee.

fixSuggestions.approach: Replace with the Initialization-on-Demand Holder idiom (static inner class)
fixSuggestions.pros: Thread-safe, lazy, and lock-free — the standard fix for this exact problem
fixSuggestions.cons: Only applies to static singleton-style fields
Every call pays lock overhead even long after the field is initialized

fixSuggestions.approach: Initialize eagerly at declaration or in the constructor
fixSuggestions.pros: Removes the race entirely; simplest possible fix if construction is cheap
fixSuggestions.cons: Loses the laziness — object is created even if never used
